methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series B → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.9x) to $400M last post money.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Drones & Autonomous (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 4 same-sector peers exist in Drones & Autonomous, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Elroy Air, a Series B drone and autonomous systems company founded by David Merrill and Clint Cope, is valued at $400M post-money after raising $116M to date. The relevant comp universe consists of 8 private firms in Drones & Autonomous, including Joby Aviation, Zipline, Nuro, Archer Aviation, and Manna Aero. No public comparables exist in this narrow sector.

The Stage-Progression method is the applicable triangulation approach here. Applied to Elroy's $400M last post-money valuation, a 1.9x step-up multiplier—representing typical Series B to next-stage progression—yields $760M. This reflects the capital intensity and development timelines characteristic of autonomous systems companies advancing toward Series C or growth-stage funding. The peer-median method could not be applied due to insufficient same-stage or adjacent-stage comparables meeting statistical thresholds; fewer than three narrow-filter peers exist to establish a credible median.

The single-method triangulation produces a valuation range of $646M (low), $760M (median), and $874M (high). Key uncertainties include the relevance of the stage-progression multiplier to Elroy's specific technical de-risking and capital deployment trajectory, the absence of directly comparable Series B exits or recent fundings in autonomous delivery, and the heterogeneity of the broader comp set across different autonomous vehicle applications. A more robust valuation would benefit from additional market data on Series B to Series C progression in hardware-dependent autonomy companies.
